name: Strands Agents
description: Open-source, model-driven AI agent SDK from AWS, used internally by Amazon Q Developer. Takes a lightweight model-first approach with MCP and A2A support, multi-agent primitives, and optional pairing with Amazon Bedrock AgentCore for hosted runtime. In June 2026 the core repo was consolidated into the strands-agents/harness-sdk monorepo (Python + TypeScript), repositioned around AWS's 'agent harness' framing; package names (strands-agents on PyPI) are unchanged.

evidence.value: Model-driven loop proven internally by Amazon Q Developer and other AWS agent teams before open-sourcing

task_completion_accuracy.methodology: Task completion testing plus review of documented internal AWS production usage

evidence.value: Native MCP client support, decorator-based Python tools, and a maintained strands-agents-tools library

tool_use_reliability.methodology: Tool invocation testing across native tools and MCP servers

evidence.value: Model-driven agentic loop delegates planning to the model; workflow and graph patterns available for structured multi-step tasks

multi_step_planning.methodology: Complex multi-step task testing with agent loop and graph patterns

evidence.value: Built-in session persistence plus integration with Amazon Bedrock AgentCore Memory for long-term memory

memory_persistence.methodology: Memory system evaluation across sessions and AgentCore Memory integration

evidence.value: Tool errors surfaced back to the model for self-correction; retry and hook system available for custom recovery

error_recovery.methodology: Error injection testing observing model-driven recovery

evidence.value: First-class multi-agent primitives: agents-as-tools, swarm, graph, and A2A protocol support

agent_collaboration.methodology: Multi-agent coordination testing across swarm, graph, and A2A patterns

evidence.value: SDK itself does not sandbox tools, but AgentCore Runtime (GA Oct 2025) provides isolated sessions and a managed Code Interpreter sandbox

tool_sandboxing.methodology: Security architecture review of SDK plus AgentCore runtime isolation

evidence.value: Integrates with AWS IAM and AgentCore Identity for credential management and per-agent permission scoping

access_control.methodology: Access control assessment of IAM and identity integrations

evidence.value: Supports Amazon Bedrock Guardrails and hook-based input/output filtering; no framework-level injection defense by default

prompt_injection_defense.methodology: Injection testing with and without Bedrock Guardrails enabled

evidence.value: AgentCore runs each session in dedicated microVM isolation; self-hosted deployments inherit user infrastructure isolation

data_isolation.methodology: Data isolation architecture review across deployment targets

evidence.value: Apache 2.0, open-sourced May 2025, public roadmap, contributions from Anthropic, Meta, and other companies
SDK retains no data itself; retention governed by user infrastructure or AWS data policies when using AgentCore

data_retention.methodology: Privacy architecture review of SDK and managed runtime options

evidence.value: Deployments on AWS inherit GDPR-aligned infrastructure controls; self-hosted deployments fully controllable

gdpr_compliance.methodology: Compliance capabilities assessment leveraging AWS compliance posture

evidence.value: Data flows only to the configured model provider (Bedrock, Anthropic, OpenAI, Ollama, LiteLLM); Bedrock keeps data within AWS

third_party_data_sharing.methodology: Data flow analysis across supported model providers

evidence.value: Runs anywhere Python/TypeScript runs, including fully local execution with Ollama models

local_deployment_option.methodology: Deployment options assessment including local model configurations

evidence.value: Thorough user guide covering concepts, safety/security, deployment, and multi-agent patterns with samples repo
Native OpenTelemetry traces, metrics, and logs built into the SDK; integrates with AgentCore Observability
Model-driven loop exposes reasoning, tool selections, and intermediate steps through traces and hooks

decision_explainability.methodology: Explainability assessment of loop transparency and hook system

evidence.value: Apache 2.0 SDK, tools, and samples; Python SDK 1.0 released 2026-05-21, TypeScript 1.0 released 2026-04-30
Core repo renamed from sdk-python to harness-sdk (June 2026) as an Apache 2.0 monorepo shipping Python and TypeScript SDKs, CLI, and docs; old sdk-python URLs redirect

open_source_code.methodology: Open source assessment of license and release maturity

evidence.source: AWS Open Source Blog / GitHub Metrics
evidence.value: 14M+ downloads by Feb 2026, rapid release cadence, and growing external contributor base

community_activity.methodology: Community engagement analysis of downloads, releases, and contributors

evidence.value: Agent in a few lines of code; model-driven approach avoids complex workflow definitions

ease_of_integration.methodology: Integration complexity assessment with minimal-setup testing

evidence.value: AgentCore Runtime (GA 2025-10-13) provides serverless, session-isolated scaling; SDK also deploys to Lambda, Fargate, EKS

scalability.methodology: Scalability assessment across managed and self-managed deployment targets

evidence.value: Free Apache 2.0 SDK; costs from LLM usage and optional AWS services (AgentCore consumption-based pricing)

cost_predictability.methodology: Pricing model analysis of SDK and optional managed services

evidence.value: Built-in OTel metrics/traces plus CloudWatch and AgentCore Observability dashboards for production monitoring

monitoring_capabilities.methodology: Monitoring features assessment across SDK and AWS integrations

evidence.value: Python 1.0 (2026-05-21) and TypeScript 1.0 (2026-04-30) with semver stability; battle-tested in Amazon Q Developer
Rapid post-1.0 cadence continues: Python SDK 1.46.0 (2026-07-08) and TypeScript 1.8.0 (2026-07-08)

production_readiness.methodology: Production readiness assessment of API stability and documented production usage

code-generation.notes: Powers Amazon Q Developer internally; strong tool use and MCP support for dev workflows

customer-support.notes: Session management, guardrails, and AgentCore identity make user-facing agents practical

data-analysis.notes: Good tool composition plus AgentCore Code Interpreter for sandboxed analysis

research-assistant.notes: Multi-agent swarm/graph patterns suit research decomposition well

financial-analysis.notes: AWS compliance posture and IAM integration help in regulated finance environments

healthcare.notes: Viable on HIPAA-eligible AWS services but requires careful architecture review

legal-compliance.notes: Strong audit trails via OTel tracing; domain guardrails must be added

best_for: Teams on AWS wanting an open SDK with a managed production runtime (AgentCore)
Enterprises needing IAM-integrated, observable agents with audit trails
Developers building multi-agent systems with MCP and A2A interoperability
Organizations seeking a battle-tested SDK proven inside Amazon Q Developer
strengths: Model-driven design proven in production by Amazon Q Developer and AWS teams
First-class MCP and A2A protocol support plus rich multi-agent primitives (swarm, graph, agents-as-tools)
Native OpenTelemetry observability built into the SDK
Truly model-agnostic: Bedrock, Anthropic, OpenAI, Ollama, LiteLLM and more
Stable 1.0 APIs in both Python and TypeScript with strong adoption (14M+ downloads)
Seamless path to managed, session-isolated runtime via Amazon Bedrock AgentCore
limitations: No built-in tool sandboxing in the SDK itself; isolation requires AgentCore or user infrastructure
Deepest integrations (Guardrails, Identity, Memory, Observability) favor the AWS ecosystem
Model-driven loop offers less deterministic control than explicit graph-first frameworks
Younger community ecosystem than longer-established agent frameworks
Advanced multi-agent patterns still maturing relative to the core single-agent loop

metadata.deployment_type: Self-hosted or managed via Amazon Bedrock AgentCore
metadata.pricing: Free (Apache 2.0) - Costs only from LLM usage and optional AWS services
metadata.adoption: 14M+ downloads by Feb 2026; used internally by Amazon Q Developer and multiple AWS teams
